paper_title: AdditiveLLM2: A Multi-modal Large Language Model for Additive Manufacturing

sections.title: Abstract
sections.content: This work presents AdditiveLLM2 a multi-modal, domain adapted large language model built upon the instruction tuned variant of the Gemma 3 model using a relatively small dataset of around 50 million tokens. The dataset (AdditiveLLM2-OA) consists of open-access additive manufacturing journal articles with data extracted for the domain adaptive pretraining and visual instruction tuning processes. Various stages of the developed model are evaluated with the Additive-Manufacturing-Benchmark which consists of additive manufacturing domain specific tasks compiled published resources. AdditiveLLM2 exhibits proficiency in both language and vision based tasks, achieving accuracies upwards of 90% in general additive manufacturing knowledge. This domain adaptive pretraining and instruction tuning strategy outline an accessible specialization method for large language models to a domain such as additive manufacturing.

sections.title: Model for Additive Manufacturing
sections.content: Peter Pak and Amir Barati Farimani,, Department of Mechanical Engineering, Carnegie Mellon University, Pittsburgh, PA, USA Machine Learning Department, Carnegie Mellon University, Pittsburgh, PA, USA E-mail: barati@cmu.edu Abstract This work presents AdditiveLLM2 multi-modal, domain adapted large language model built upon the instruction tuned variant of the Gemma 3 model using relatively small dataset of around 50 million tokens. The dataset (AdditiveLLM2-OA) consists of open-access additive manufacturing journal articles with data extracted for the domain adaptive pretraining and visual instruction tuning processes. Various stages of the developed model are evaluated with the Additive-Manufacturing-Benchmark which consists of additive manufacturing domain specific tasks compiled published resources. AdditiveLLM2 exhibits proficiency in both language and vision based tasks, achieving accuracies upwards of 90% in general additive manufacturing knowledge. This domain adaptive pretraining and instruction tuning strategy outline an accessible specialization method for large language models to domain such as additive manufacturing.

sections.title: Introduction
sections.content: Large language Models (LLMs) have exhibited proficient capability for knowledge based tasks in fields extending beyond that of natural language processing such as chemistry, 13 design, 47 1 mathematics, 811 robotics, 1216 and software development. 1719 Within Additive Manufacturing (AM), LLMs have been applied to various tasks such as knowledge retrieval, 20,21 parameter selection, 22,23 and process optimization. 24 When applied to agentic systems, the reasoning and strategic planning capabilities of LLMs are particularly useful for enabling the intelligent automation of complex tasks such as drug discovery, 25 alloy evaluation, 23 print optimization, 24 and code debugging 19 to name few. To more efficiently execute these specialized tasks, an LLM aware of the complexities and discoveries within the selected field is desired. 2628 To this point, domain adapted LLMs have number of unique advantages over generally purpose LLMs namely the efficiency in which valid responses can be generated by drawing on parametric (within training set) data. 26,28 Non-parametric data can be injected into the LLMs response generation process through the use Retrieval Augmented Generation (RAG) based architectures. 29 This is powerful approach that is capable of generating results grounded in factual data, 20,21 however, with each request the retrieved passages consume additional context within the conversation window. For dynamic data (events, machine logs, database updates, etc.) RAG enables up-to-date responses without additional pretraining. 29 The medium for domain knowledge is generally static (journal articles, figures, textbooks, etc.) and the extra training cost done with Domain Adaptive Pretraining (DAPT) enables accurate response generation without additional context consumption seen with RAG. 26 Another consideration is the local use and deployment of these LLMs as seen in cases where national security, 30,31 patient data, 3234 or environmental limitations 15 deem an edge or onpremise solution necessary. In this work, domain adapted LLM for additive manufacturing (AdditiveLLM2) is created with the multi-modal Gemma 3 (12B) model 35  (Fig. 1)  . Domain adaptive pretraining and visual instruction tuning is performed using text and image data from various openaccess AM journal articles curated within the AdditiveLLM2-OA dataset. 36 This work also introduces the Additive-Manufacturing-Benchmark which measures the LLMs specific ca2 pabilities in tasks such as melt pool dimensional prediction, FDM defect identification, and general knowledge about AM. The methods and architecture used in developing AdditiveLLM2 showcase how large language models can be efficiently tailored to provide enhanced domain knowledge within given field.

sections.title: 2 Related Work
sections.content: Previous works have explored the application of large language models to solving challenges within the domain of additive manufacturing. These include approaches such as in-context learning, 41 fine-tuning, 22 retrieval augmented generation, 20,21 the use of vision language models, 4244 and the development of agentic systems. 23,24 To the best of the authors knowledge there exists no work which investigates the adaptation of large language models to domain of additive manufacturing within both the language and vision space through the use of continual pre-training and instruction tuning. The original AdditiveLLM 22 paper investigated the fine-tuning of various different pretrained large language models and evaluated these models on their prediction accuracy regarding the classification task of process map defect regimes. Data used for this fine-tuning process was obtained from the MeltpoolNet dataset 38 and FLOW-3D based simulations. This dataset was used to fine-tune models ranging in size from 60 million parameters to billion parameters: DistilBERT (66M), 45 SciBERT (110M), 46 T5-Small (60M), 47 and Llama 3.2 (1B). 48 An accuracy of 82% was achieved when predicting defect regimes within laser powder bed fusion (keyholing, lack of fusion, balling, or none) given natural language formatted process parameters using the fine-tuned DistilBERT model. 22 An in-context learning approach to enable large language models to detect defects encountered during the vat photopolymerization process was explored by Fang et al. 41 The authors utilize camera mounted to the underside of the resin vat to obtain images of the resin deposition process, looking for defects such as debris and unfilled streaks within the 3 Figure 1: General training and evaluation process utilized in the development of AdditiveLLM2. (1) Domain adaptive pretraining (DAPT) is applied in both the text and vision modalities using the Gemma 3 model using selection of open-access articles from the additive manufacturing domain. 35 as its base. (2) Visual Instruction Tuning (VIT) utilizes the extracted captions from these articles and generates description and conversation prompts with the aid of GPT-OSS 120B. 37 The domain adapted model is evaluated with tasks from the Additive-Manufacturing-Benchmark which are compiled from various published resources. 3840 4 build platform. 41 The taken images are then provided to GPT-4o, along with positive and negative image samples, in order to predict whether the current layer is normal or defective. 41 These samples, along with text descriptions of both cases, provides additional contextual information to guide the large language model to predict the correct outcome only through information provided within the conversations context window. By coupling the language and image descriptions of each case, this in-context learning method can distinguish between normal and defective build layers and achieved 96% classification accuracy. In AMGPT by Chandrasekhar et al. 20 explores the use of Retrieval Augmented Generation (RAG) 29 specifically within the application of additive manufacturing. RAG at its core is based on the cosine similarity between the query and document passages, often utilizing separate encoders for the document passages and query text to compare the two within the same embedding space. 29 The authors utilize this dual-encoder retrieval mechanism to obtain the relevant passages for given specific user query and these passages are provided along with the query as input to the pretrained Llama2-7B 49 model for response generation. This work showcases the effectiveness of utilizing empirical data within the generation process of large language model as the authors make the claim that their RAG enabled response displayed less factual errors than that of GPT-4. 20 Similar to this, work by Naghavi Khanghah et al. 21 extends the use of RAG into vision space and leverages multimodal approach for the detection and classification of anomalies specifically within laser powder bed fusion. With over 50 test images, measured anomalies of recoater hopping, recoater streaking, incomplete spreading, swelling, debris, and soot were classified. Through the use of models such as Qwen2-VL-2B and GPT-4o-mini, the authors demonstrate that the utilization of the RAG based system was reported to improve the accuracy of model prediction by around 12%. 21 The domain specific capabilities of these large language models can then be utilized in agentic systems as their multi-modal and reasoning abilities are quite suitable for the orchestration of tool calls and actions. This approach can be utilized in tasks such as 5 alloy evaluation for additive manufacturing where based on material properties calculated by Thermo-Calc, the potential for lack of fusion porosity can be evaluated for composition of elements. 23 Another application of agents is shown in LLM-3D Print by Jadhav et al. which explores the use of multi-agent system for the detection and mitigation of defects within the Fused Deposition Modeling (FDM) process. These prints were evaluated with compression testing and showed that the agentic system helped enable clear improvements in mechanical performance. 24 In both of these works, the agentic system relied on off-theshelf large language models (i.e. GPT-4o, Claude, Gemini). 23,24 However, it has yet to be explored that domain adapted, fine-tuned model for additive manufacturing within these agentic systems would exhibit enhanced performance.

sections.title: 3.1 Large Language Models
sections.content: A Large Language Model (LLM) is neural network which commonly utilizes transformer based architectures trained to the task of next token prediction. 50,51 This type of model is often pretrained on corpus of natural language data ranging from Wikipedia articles to code available on GitHub and showcases its comprehension of these datasets through various benchmarking tasks. 26,52,53 Adhering to scaling laws, these models often exhibit improved performance with larger parameter counts, compute times, and dataset size. 54 Furthermore, these models can operate beyond the bounds of natural language through different architectural modifications which allows for the interpretation of images, 55 videos, 13 and 3d models. 56 3.1.1 Transformer Architecture Before the inception of the transformer architecture, 50 long short-term memory 57 and gated recurrent neural networks 58 were the predominant approaches to solve language and sequence modeling tasks. However, these previous approaches were limited in their lack of 6 parallelization and constrained context window as they struggled to model long range dependencies. 50,57,58 The transformer architecture, in contrast, primarily relying upon the attention mechanism to model long range dependencies bypassing the need for convolution or recurrent mechanisms. 50 Within the original transformer model, the architecture consists of two stacks: the encoder stack and the decoder stack. 50 The encoder stack consists of bidirectional self attention where contextual representation can be generated by attending to entirely of input tokens. 50 The decoder is concerned with next token generation as it can only attend to the previous tokens within the output sequence. 50 Implementations of this encoder-decoder transformer architecture include models such as T5 47 and BART, 59 which excelled in fixed output tasks such as summarization and translation. Encoder only models such as BERT 51 and RoBERTa 60 utilize leverage bidirectional attention to generate comprehensive embedding space for dense retrieval, particularly useful in domain specific representation environments such as those covered in CatBERTa, 61 SciBERT, 46 and. 56 However, decoder only transformer stacks such as GPT 62 have evolved to become the dominant approach as it scales better for generative and reasoning tasks focused next token prediction. 3.1.2 Multi-modal Input Representation The transformer architecture can be applied to multi-modal tasks through modifications within the tokenization (Appendix A) process allowing for an effective representation of visual images 13,55 and 3D models. 56 Text is provided to the transformer as 1 dimensional input vectors and naively flattening 2 or 3 dimensional data often produces inadequate representations, often resulting in lost temporal and spatial data. 13,55 An approach to preserving spatial data is outlined in work by Dosovitskiy et al. 55 where the authors split an input image into fixed patches while applying positional embedding in their Vision Transformer (ViT). This is further expanded upon by Arnab et al. 13 where these patches are expanded in an additional dimension for video frames to embed spatial and temporal information into 7 the input. 13 For 3D models, point cloud representation is an efficient means of representing spatial information without the rigid constraints of voxelization. However, the unstructured format of point clouds presents challenge when adapting this data to be suitable for transformer input as the tokenization process for such representation is not immediately obvious. 56 Point-BERT 56 resolves this issue by partitioning the entire 3D model into point based patches similar to the previously methologies of ViT. 55 These patches of points referred to as subclouds preserves the spatial information necessary for adequately mapping 3D model data in an format comprehensible by the transformer architecture. 56 3.1.3 Model Scaling With the increasing parameter size of models developed with the transformer architecture, emergent behaviors such as reasoning become evident. 6365 This evolution in scale can be attributed to number of factors such as the parallelization of self-attention computations, regularization of model weights through residual connections, and the shift to decoder focused transformer stacks. 50,62 This finding is validated by Kaplan et al. 64 where model performance depends heavily upon number of parameters, size of dataset, and the amount of compute used during training. This work established the existence of power law relationship between performance and factors such as parameter size (768 to 1.5B), dataset size (22M - 23B), and compute (105 to 103 PetaFlop days).

sections.title: 3.2 Prompting and Reasoning
sections.content: 3.2.1 Chain-of-Thought Chain-of-Thought (CoT) is multi-step prompting technique to elicit further developed answers from the large language model than simple standard prompting. 66,67 In this method, the prompt is formatted in manner such that step-by-step answer is provided to an example question before similar question is posed in the input. 66,67 This facilitates reasoning within the model as it decomposes the prompt into multi-step problem which allows for additional computations be allocated to these individual steps. 67 For example, while constructing the prompt rather than simply stating the direct answer to given problem, the answer is formatted in way to provide the granular steps taken to arrive at an answer 67  (Fig. 9)  . This method is particularly useful in facilitating fidelity in multi-step arithmetic problems along with providing interpretable insight into reasoning within the LLM. In addition to formatted user prompts, CoT reasoning provides useful avenue to monitor large language model outputs for potential exploits that may produce misaligned behavior output. 68 This has been shown with the monitoring of verbose CoT outputs from larger models (i.e. o3-mini) using weaker models (i.e. GPT-4o) to prevent reward hacking schemes. 68 For example, Baker et al. 68 highlights an example where by monitoring the CoT of models trajectory using separate agent, reward hacking scheme of modifying unit tests to always pass is thwarted. This proves useful in directing the model to complete tasks using the correct approach rather than choosing the simpler, often incorrect, approach. However, the authors have found that given too much optimization the model can learn hide its intent within the CoT producing avenues where in which hallucination can occur. 37,68 3.2.2 Zero-Shot With the increasing size of Large Language Models, Zero-Shot reasoning has been shown to be sufficient in eliciting deeper thought responses without the need for step-by-step examples. 66 Rather, simple addition to the prompt such as Lets think step by step would be sufficient in encouraging the model to produce more well formed answer. 66 This enables minimalist approach to probe for complex reasoning with the large language model leveraging the large corpus of data that the model has been trained on. 54,66 This type of reasoning is often baked into the large language model with fine-tuning method called Instruction Tuning (Section 3.3.3). 63 Wei et al. 63 utilizes this technique to further train large language models with Natural Language Instruction templates to bet9 ter elicit stronger inference capability from the model. In the developed 137B parameter Finetuned Language Net (FLAN) model, the authors find that FLANs zero-shot performance outperformed the zero-shot performance of the 175B parameter GPT-3 in over 80% of evaluations. 63 3.2.3 ReAct ReAct (Reason + Act) is general paradigm that combines reasoning and actions within the large language model to utilize feedback to make informed choices for the next set of actions. 69 By utilizing prompt based approach to navigating through an action space, ReAct is able to update its current policy by reasoning over its current context and observations. This is achieved by decomposing given task into smaller set of steps similar to the Chainof-Thought process. 67,69 At given timestep (t), each step consists of language space action (ˆat) which Yao et al. 69 refer to as thought or reasoning trace, an environmental action (at) such as tool call, and an observation (ot) which is the result of action (at). The LLM generates policy (π(atct)) for the next action (at) given the current context (ct) which consists of all actions and observations from previous timesteps. language space action or aforementioned thought is performed to update the context (ct+1 = (ct, ˆat)) allowing for dynamic policies which can be adjusted with feedback. 69 Each step is composed of Thought, Action and Observation which the LLM is prompted to complete. 69 The Thought is the language space action that the LLM produces to create the updated context from the existing context space after both an Action and Observation are performed. 69 Actions are then performed by parsing the subsequent output from the LLM to search for tools that match specific syntax (i.e. search[entity], lookup[string], or finish[answer]). The respective function is then executed with the provided argument producing an Observation which is then appended to the context before moving onto the next step. This Thought, Action and Observation process is repeated until either the LLM produces an Action consisting of finish[answer] or an iteration limit is reached. 69 During this process, the CoT reasoning is visible throughout each step providing transparency into the mechanisms used to construct the final answer.

sections.title: 3.3 Domain Adaptation
sections.content: Large Language Models are pretrained on corpus of available data with modalities in natural language text, 37,54 general images, 7072 and video sequences. 13,70 Pretraining these models on diverse set of data builds general knowledge and reasoning capabilities useful for generating comprehensible responses for user queries. 54 The general knowledge embedded into the model from pretraining can be leveraged and further adapted to specialize in specific applications or downstream tasks through methods such as domain adaptive pretraining, such as those in biology, chemistry, and other fields. 26,46,61,73 Low-Rank Adaptation is common approach to injecting this domain knowledge into the large language model without retraining all of the model parameters, effectively utilizing available resources and optimizing on memory and computation. 74 Through supervised fine-tuning, the behavior of the large language model can be adjusted to further align with its downstream application via methods such as instruction tuning. 43, 3.3.1 Domain-Adaptive Pretraining Domain-Adaptive Pretraining (DAPT) within large language models continues the next token prediction self-supervised training process by utilizing smaller, yet focused set of data. 26,7577 For instance, the subsequent dataset for DAPT could include text from research papers, 26 textual representations of atoms, 61 or multi-domain scientific papers. 46 Gururangan et al. 26 explores the application of DAPT in the domains of BioMed (2.68M papers), CS (2.22M papers), 78 News (11.90M articles), 79 and Reviews (2.475M reviews) 80 on the RoBERTa 60 model for single pass on each dataset. The authors observe that DAPT generates improved responses over the base RoBERTa 60 model in all domains, particularly in the BioMed, CS, and Reviews domain showcasing the benefits such as an approach has when 11 the source domain of the model is distance from the target domain of the model. 3.3.2 Low-Rank Adaptation With the increasing scale of parameters in large language models, adjusting each parameter via fine-tuning becomes prohibitively expensive. 74 As of writing, many popular large language models such as GPT-3 (175 B), 74 GPT-OSS (20B and 120B), 37 Llama 4 (109B) 81 surpass 100 billion parameters, with expectations to scale to over 1 trillion trainable parameters. 82 Pretraining alone for these models can take upwards of several months and retraining each model to specific application evolves from an inconvenient task to an infeasible endeavor. 74 This growing inaccessibility of retraining all parameters of large language models to specific domain establishes need for more efficient method approach to fine-tuning. To this end, consideration towards the number of effective parameters is investigated as adjusting just these parameters would be enough to sufficiently adapt the large language model to specific domain. 74,83,84 This is referred by the intrinsic dimension, providing measurement for the minimum number of parameters that is necessary for model to produce satisfactory responses to an objective function. 83,84 With this, Aghajanyan et al. was able to achieve 90% of the expected performance on sematic equivalency binary classification task on the RoBERTa model 60 by training only select 200 parameters. This finding is the foundation that Low-Rank Adaption (LoRA) is built upon, proposing an approach that can reduce the number of trainable parameters by up to factor of 10, with only just third of the training memory requirement. 74 In this approach (Figure 2), the gradients of the pretrained weight matrix W0 are kept frozen the accumulated gradients are respresented by their low-rank decomposition BA (W = BA). Rdr and Rrk. 74 Both and are dense layers which contain trainable parameters, is the dimension of the transformer layer of the model, is the input dimension of the weight matrix, and is the rank that satisfies the condition < min(d, k). 74 In the adapter, the weights for are initialized using gaussian distribution = (0, σ2) with weights for 12 set to 0. 74 Figure 2: Original figure by Hu et al. 74 diagrams LoRA for square pretrained weight matrix (d d). Figure is adapted to showcase an example of pretrained weight matrix with different input and output dimensions (d k). Hu et al. 74 applies this approach only to within the self attention portion of GPT-2 (Wq, Wk, Wv, and Wo). Within the backwards pass only the adapter weights are updated, original pretrained weights are left frozen. 74 The forward pass is modified from = W0x to = W0x+W , adding the results from the trained adapter to the output of the frozen weights. 74 For inference, the pretrained weights and the adapter weights can be added together = W0 + BA, removing any introduction of latency and furthermore can be easily switched out for different adapter at minimal overhead cost. 74 3.3. Instruction Tuning Instruction Tuning (IT) is type of fine tuning which further aligns the LLM to produce better question answering responses. 43,63 This process utilizes labeled datasets in supervised learning environment to optimize the prediction performance of the model to the corresponding label for specific input. 43,63 Wei et al. 63 investigates the effectiveness of IT on zero-shot (Section 3.2.2) prompts to understand whether the model will exhibit unseen task performance when fine-tuning on collection tasks. The authors find that performance improvements scale with the number of IT tasks and also denote that specific model parameter size is necessary to realize these gains. 63 For models under specific parameter size (8B), IT has been observed to hinder task performance potentially due to the limited model capacity which instruction tuning then completely consumes. 63 The IT FLAN model was shown to outperform the GPT-3 model in number of unseen tasks and showcases the potential for even larger LLMs to perform well in zero-shot question answering prompts. 63 This practice can be extended to visual domain with Visual Instruction Tuning (VIT). 43 In work by Liu et al., 43 the authors take multi-modal approach to IT in their Large Language and Vision Assistant (LLaVA) model achieving an accuracy of 92.53% when evaluating on the Science QA dataset. 65 The images obtained 85 from COCO include both caption and object localization which are then utilized in generating the three IT formats: Conversation, Detailed Description, and Complex Reasoning. 43 The Conversation format (58K samples) utilizes only the caption text to generate 3 question and answer responses with the assistance of GPT-4 for each image. The Detailed description (23K samples) utilizes the questions from the previous format to also prompt GPT-4 for comprehensive description of the image. 43 Lastly, the Complex Reasoning (77K samples) format elicit more step-by-step answers to given question. 43 Liu et al. 43 chose the Vicuna 86 model (13B) to perform their IT experiments upon. Within their ablation studies it was found that without any instruction tuning, the model performed poorly within all categories, demonstrating the effectiveness of IT. 43 By just adding in Conversation to the IT dataset, significant improvement gains are observed with evaluations across all three IT formats by minimum of 30 points and the incorporation of the entire IT dataset increases the score by 50 points across the board. 43 When compared to GPT-4 the multi-modal capabilities of LLaVA produces relative score of around 85% on instruction-following dataset examples.

sections.title: 4 Methodology
sections.content: AdditiveLLM2 is large language model adapted to the field of additive manufacturing using domain adaptive pretraining (Section 3.3.1) and instruction tuning (Section 3.3.3). The 12 billion parameter instruction tuned variant of the Gemma 3 model 35 is used as the base as it provides multi-modal architecture capable of performing inference upon natural language and visual inputs. The base model is trained on both the text and images extracted from various open-access additive manufacturing journal articles including Journal of Additive Manufacturing, Additive Manufacturing Letters, Journal of Manufacturing Processes, and Rapid Prototyping Journal. Visual instruction tuning examples are created from the extract data using the 120 billion parameter GPT-OSS model which along with the extracted text and images are uploaded and hosted HuggingFace as the AdditiveLLM2-OA dataset. 36 The development of the model is evaluated with the Additive-Manufacturing-Benchmark dataset consisting of general knowledge questions regarding additive manufacturing, visual identification tasks, and other data based prediction tasks.

sections.title: 4.1 Model
sections.content: The adapted model is expected to provide enhanced domain expertise within the field of additive manufacturing. To achieve this the LLM will also need to utilize vision transformer in order to function beyond the scope of natural language as additive manufacturing tasks are often multi-modal challenge. Vision based approaches have been utilized within the space of additive manufacturing for tasks such as porosity prediction, 44,87 melt pool estimation, 88 and build monitoring. 24 Open weight LLMs from several frontier labs were considered for this work, these include those from OpenAI, 37 Meta, 81 and Google. 35 The 20 billion parameter variant of GPT-OSS 37 offers promising foundation for AdditiveLLM2, however its architecture is constrained to the medium of text. Llama 4 Scout 81 is multi-modal LLM and utilizes mixture of elements architecture with 17 billion active parameters and 16 experts. 15 Although only 17 billion parameters are active at given time, 109 billion parameters still need to be loaded into memory just for inference, exceeding the hardware capacity of this experimental setup. The Gemma 3 family of models 35 provides suitable balance between functionality and memory consumption. For this reason the 12 billion parameter Gemma 3 model was selected as the base for AdditiveLLM2 and domain adapted to tasks within the field of additive manufacturing. 4.1.1 Gemma The Gemma 3 family of models 35 offers wide selection of models that can operate on consumer grade hardware with comparable performance (in the 27B variant evaluated with the Chatbot Arena 89 rating system) to other models such as DeepSeek-R1, 90 Qwen2.5Max, 91 ChatGPT-4o, and Claude 3.7 Sonnet. Four different variants of the Gemma 3 model are available those being 1B, 4B, 12B, and 27B parameter configurations, all capable of multi-modal text and image inference with the exception of the 1B parameter variant. 35 The Gemma 3 models utilize decoder-only transformer architecture and the 400M variant of the SigLIP vision encoder (frozen) 92 for handling visual inputs, composing separate vision and language stacks. 35 Pretraining of the Gemma 3 family of models was achieved through process known as knowledge distillation, 93 supervised learning technique where smaller student model is trained to mimic the behavior of the more capable teacher model. Specific to the 12 billion parameter variant of Gemma 3, total of 12 trillion tokens were used during the pretraining process consisting of images, text, and multilingual data to improve language coverage. 35 Post training procedures also utilize knowledge distillation for instruction tuning tasks along with improved variations of reinforcement learning techniques such as BOND, 94 WARM, 95 and WARP. 96 When compared to other variants within the Gemma 3 family, the 12B IT variant performs near to that of the 27B IT and significantly better than the immediately lower 4B IT variant on various general reasoning and understanding benchmarks. 35,

sections.title: 4.2 Dataset
sections.content: The multi-modal dataset (AdditiveLLM2-OA hosted on HuggingFace 36) created for domain adaptation was sourced from four different peer reviewed journals: Journal of Additive Manufacturing, Additive Manufacturing Letters, Journal of Manufacturing Processes, and Rapid Prototyping Journal. Text, images, and visual instruction tuning (VIT) examples compiled for this dataset utilized all articles (1,704 total) published within these journals under the open-access license up to February of 2026. Among the keywords associated with these articles, Laser Powder Bed Fusion as the most common followed by other processes such as material extrusion, directed energy deposition, and vat photopolymerization  (Fig. 12)  . Composition (text, images, VIT) of each configuration by journal with Journal of Additive Manufacturing accounting for majority of each configuration followed by Journal of Manufacturing Processes  (Fig. 3)  . Text was obtained from these 1,704 articles and amounts to around 29 million tokens with Rapid Prototyping Journal and Journal of Additive Manufacturing exhibiting the most distinct vocabulary from one another (Fig. 10 and Fig. 11). Figure 3: Composition of each dataset configuration by journal for (left) text, (center) images, and (right) visual instruction tuning (VIT). The reduced Rapid Prototyping Journal slice in the VIT configuration (right) is due to the smaller number of figure and caption pairs found within that journal during the data extraction process. All articles were downloaded from their respective journals in the .pdf format and using the PyMuPDF 103 library, the relevant text and images were extracted from each file. The data 17 extraction process for each assumes consistent content structure in order to parse attributes such as authors, keywords, and figure captions. In the few cases these attributes are not parsed properly author names are incomplete, keywords are left out, or figures captions are left empty. This issue mostly affects small number of the early articles from each journal as the latter issues adopt consistent formatting. Figure 4: Content (text) and figures (images) are extracted from their .pdf formats from open-access articles from various additive manufacturing journals. This data is utilized for domain adaptive pretraining and the figure captions are processed into question answer conversations and detailed descriptions using GPT-OSS 37 for visual instruction tuning. The various data configurations are uploaded and publicly accessible via HuggingFace. Over 24,000 images were extracted for this dataset  (Fig. 3)  amounting to over 6 million image tokens and over 10 million text tokens for their respective captions. 36 These images were primarily obtained from article figures with consideration towards maintaining the association between the caption text. These pairs would be utilized in the domain adaptive pretraining process of the model utilizing images  (Fig. 5)  . Following the practices outlined by Liu et al. 43 in their work for visual instruction tuning, detailed descriptions and conversation examples were generated using the extracted image captions. local deployment of the GPT-OSS (120B) model 37 was used to generate the detailed description and question-answer 18 conversation examples using only the figures caption as input. If figures image-caption pair was not properly extracted, its VIT examples were not generated. This provides an explanation why Rapid Prototyping Journal occupies smaller fraction of the VIT dataset configuration when compared to the other configurations of text or images  (Fig. 3)  . Around 20,000 VIT examples were compiled amounting to total of around 12 million text tokens and 5 million image tokens. 36 In total the dataset consists of around 57 million tokens: 45 million of which are text and the other 11 million that are image.

sections.title: 4.3 Training
sections.content: Training for AdditiveLLM2 was split into three sequential stages: Domain Adaptive Pretraining (Text), Domain Adaptive Pretraining (Images), and Visual Instruction Tuning (Conversation and Detailed Description). This is outlined in Figure 5 where the Gemma 3 35 model is used as the base for text and image based DAPT using the content extracted from various open-access AM articles, further instruction tuned with generated input-label pairs from the extracted figures. The text, images, and vit configurations of the AdditiveLLM2-OA dataset 104 hosted on HuggingFace is used for each respective stage in the training process. Each of the model weights are adapted using LoRA (Section 3.3.2) with the corresponding rank (r = 16) and alpha (a = 32) configurations applied to the query, key, value, and output projection layers within self-attention blocks. Each stage of the training process was performed for 3 epochs on machine consisting of 3 Nvidia A6000 GPUs for duration of around 36 hours per stage. Domain adaptive pretraining extends the pretraining process using unlabeled data for the task of next token prediction (Section 3.3.1). The first stage of the training process applies DAPT to the language modality of the model, adapting the corresponding attention weights utilizing the vocabulary, concepts, and phrasing within additive manufacturing articles. Training inputs during this stage have split of 95% train and 5% validation and are provided in chunks of 2048 tokens from the text configuration of AdditiveLLM2-OA. 19 Figure 5: Gemma 3 12B variants (pretrained only or instruction tuned) are adapted to the domain of additive manufacturing using LoRA in 3 step training process. (1) Extracted text from the open-access articles is used to train language model attention weights for next token prediction. (2) Extracted figures along with their respective captions are utilized to train the vision tower attention weights while keeping the language model weights frozen. (3) Visual instruction tuning post training on all attention weights is performed using questionanswer pairs along with detailed image descriptions. 20 The weights of the LLM are adapted using LoRA and merged into the base weights for the next stage of the training process. The second stage utilizes the figures extracted from each of the articles to adapt the vision tower of the LLM. In this stage, the attention weights of the previous language modality are frozen as the intent is to train the model to build an associate between figure images and caption text. The last stage of the training process applies the post-training technique of instruction tuning (Section 3.3.3) to fine-tune the LLMs performance in additive manufacturing related tasks. labeled dataset of conversation question-answer pairs and detailed descriptions generated from the extracted figures are used to perform supervised fine-tuning (SFT). In this stage, LoRA is performed to adapt both the language and vision weights of the model. For the description task, the model is prompted to provide description of the given input image and the loss is calculated between generated response and the ground truth. In the multi-turn conversation task, the Gemma 3 specific <start of turn> token 35 is utilized to generate question answer conversation of given input image. Within this conversation, 3 questions are provided with the loss only computed on the response to the last question as to allow the first 2 questions to build context within the conversation. Both methods implement prompt masking which incentivizes the model to generate responses to given questions. The maximum length of token outputs in this stage is increased from 768 to 1024 to accommodate the longer responses expected during this stage. This work investigates the multi-stage training process on variants of the base Gemma 3 12B model with (gemma-3-12b-it) and without (gemma-3-12b-pt) additional instruction tuning. Within the instruction tuned variant of the base model, the LLM is already proficient in responding to user prompts and is capable of generating structured responses, however the base model with only pretraining has not been aligned to generate ideal responses to conversational tasks. Evaluating performance on both variants will provide the data necessary for an ablation study of the training process at its various stages.

sections.title: 4.4 Benchmarking
sections.content: To properly evaluate the capabilities of each domain adapted model, benchmark consisting of additive manufacturing related questions was created and accessible through the additive-manufacturing package. 104 Additive-Manufacturing-Benchmark  (Fig. 6)  aims to provide comprehensive assessment in both the language and visual modalities encountered within the field of additive manufacturing, consisting of several AM tasks evaluating general process knowledge, defect recognition, and other adjacent capabilities. Sourcing of the data used within these benchmarking tasks originate from domain expert generated course materials and published datasets. 3840 The tasks for general knowledge regarding additive manufacturing are delivered in two forms: short answer and multiple choice (Fig. 6a and 6b). In both formats there are 127 questions covering various processes from laser powder bed fusion, binder jet, directed energy deposition, and etc. The multiple choice format provides 4 different choices with one correct answer and the short answer provides rubric to evaluate short answer questions. The short answer format utilizes the GPT-OSS 20B model 37 to award points based off the rubric where maximum score of 127 points can be achieved for this task. The melt pool prediction task (Fig. 6c) provides metric to evaluate an LLMs familiarity with resulting melt pool dimensions associated with configuration of process parameters such as beam power, scanning velocity, or material. This task utilizes experimental data from MeltpoolNet 38 where melt pool dimensions along with the prescribed process parameters are compiled into cohesive dataset. This task aims to probe the model to provide estimations of melt pool dimensions, in the unit of microns, of the depth, length, and width given combination of process parameters. The predictions are evaluated using RMSE where value closer to 0 is desired. The Fused Deposition Modeling (FDM) defect prediction accuracy task (Fig. 6d) evaluates the models visual capability by prompting the model to correctly assign defect classification to given FDM process image. These images are obtained from Hu et al. 40 22 Figure 6: Additive-Manufacturing-Benchmark is set of multi-modal tasks compiled from various published resources 3840 used to evaluate AM comprehension. Language based tasks include answering general knowledge questions in (a) short answer or (b) multiple choice Image-based formats and (c) melt pool dimension prediction given process parameters. tasks including (d) FDM Defect identification, (e) AM machine identification, and (f ) LPBF anomaly identification. 23 and provide defect classifications for in-situ build images include warping, stringing, cracking, layer shift, and off platform. total of 100 samples are provided to the LLM and the model is evaluated on binary task of assigning the image to the correct defect classification. The machines identification task (Fig. 6e) also evaluates the ability of the LLM to recognize an image of given machine. This is intended to evaluate the models ability to generally understand what AM machines look like and what process each is associated with. The reasoning behind including this task is gather insight into the incorporation of article figures into the models vision stack as many articles include descriptions and images of the equipment utilized for their experiments. The model is expected to provide prediction for the associated process, manufacturing company, and name of the machine provided in the input image. Half the response weight is placed on the correct identification of the AM process associated with the machine featured in the image with the latter two questions (name and manufacturer) accounting for the remaining response weight. The LPBF anomaly identification task (Fig. 6f) with the Peregrine dataset 39 aims to evaluate capability of the LLM to recognize build anomalies within laser powder bed fusion. More specifically, this visual identification task asks the model to classify which anomalies exist on given build layer after melting. These anomalies are obtained using the Peregrine software and include classifications such as recoater hopping, under melting, over melting, spatter, debris, and etc. Task performance is measured using an F1 score which considers both the precision and recall of set of predictions ranging from worst case of 0 and best case of 1 (Equation 1). Recall = P + Precision = P + F1 = 2 2 + + (1)

sections.title: 5 Results and Discussion
sections.content: The various stages of the AdditiveLLM2 domain adaptation process was evaluated with tasks from the Additive-Manufacturing-Benchmark performed over set of 5 trials. Models developed from the instruction tuned (Gemma-3-12b-it) and solely pretrained (Gemma-3-12bpt) variant of Gemma-3 35 were investigated at DAPT text, DAPT images, and VIT training iterations and compared against the base model. For all cases it was observed that additional pretraining and instruction tuning enabled further domain knowledge specialization as the base model was not selected as the best model for specific task in any of the cases (Fig 7 and 8). In most tasks, models adapted on the variant of Gemma 3 with only pretraining performed significantly worse than their instruction tuned counterparts.

sections.title: 5.1 Benchmark Results
sections.content: Specific to the models using the instruction tuned base model  (Fig. 7)  , these on average showcased the best performance especially in the task of General Knowledge Multiple Choice. Within this task of 127 question, the base IT model already achieves an impressive score of around 88% accuracy and with domain adaptive pretraining, the performance increases to around 93% accuracy. The short answer format of the general knowledge task indicates similar trend however significantly lower performance as the maximum achievable score is 127. For the prediction of melt pool dimensions, the model with only DAPT text training performs the best with subsequent tests for DAPT images and VIT progressively increasing in average RMSE. The final VIT stage of the model performs the best in visual tasks in LPBF anomaly identification and machine identification with the exception of FDM defect identification. With models developed from the base Gemma 3 model 35 with only pretraining applied  (Fig. 8)  , the performance on all tasks were often significantly worse than those from the instruction tuned base. For these cases, visual instruction tuned models showcased the 25 Figure 7: The visual instruction tuned version of Gemma-3-12b-it exhibits the best performance in most image based tasks and closely follow the performance of the base model with only domain adaptation in text for remaining tasks. best performance in all tasks with the exception of LPBF anomaly identification where the performance seems to decrease with additional training stages. This underlines the impact that instruction tuning has on the general usability of an LLM and the effect that visual instruction tuning can have to specific domain. One trend seen within all tasks for both the PT and IT variants of the base model show that performance at the DAPT image training stage noticeably decreases. This is seen most in language based tasks such as general knowledge and in few image based tasks such as LPBF anomaly identification. Contributing factors to this could be the freezing of language attention weights during the DAPT image stage, some images not having associated caption pairs, or catastrophic forgetting. 26 Figure 8: All models adapted upon the Gemma-3-12b-pt (pretraining only) variant of the base model exhibit comparatively worse performance to their Gemma-3-12b-it variants. Models at the visual instruction tuned achieve the best performance and in some cases outperform their previous training stages by significant factor.

sections.title: 5.2 Domain Specialization Performance
sections.content: In work by Gururangan et al., 26 the authors perform domain adaptive pretraining on the RoBERTA-base model (around 100 million parameters) 60 for around 1 epoch using dataset consisting of around 24B tokens those from BioMed (7.55B), CS (8.10B), News (6.66B), and Reviews (2.11B). In the domain adaptation of AdditiveLLM2, the total token count for text based training amount to just around 45M, considerably lower than 2B even with 3 epochs of training (135M). Yet, with this lower amount of tokens, the final vision instruction tuned AdditiveLLM2 model (from gemma-3-12b-it base) shows improved performance on tasks such as general knowledge short answer, LPBF anomaly detection, FDM defect detection, and machine recognition over the base model  (Fig. 7)  . An explanation for this performance could be attributed to the more narrow scope that the domain of additive manufacturing that AdditiveLLM2 apply to in comparison to the more general domains of biology, medicine, computer science, journalism, and consumer reviews found in Gururangan et al. 26 Wang et al. 105 used dataset of the same size (48.5M) and were able to achieve improved results over their base model with similar pretraining and fine-tuning process. More so, Junior et al. 106 observed an inverse trend with the required compute needed for domain adaptation to the size of the model. With this, it would still be interesting to explore the effect that more comprehensive additive manufacturing dataset would have to its performance.

sections.title: 6 Conclusion
sections.content: In this work, AdditiveLLM2 is shown to outperform its pretrained and instruction tuned base counterparts of the Gemma 3 model 35 after stages of domain adaptive pretraining and visual instruction tuning. The model trained on selection of open-access additive manufacturing articles establishes that domain adaptation is possible on relatively small dataset of around 45 million tokens over 3 epochs for each stage. Evaluated on AM benchmarking tasks, the visual instruction tuned variant of AdditiveLLM2 (built upon Gemma-3-12b-it) exhibits the best performance in vision based tasks and language tasks, achieving accuracy in general knowledge upwards of 90%. With this, domain adaptive pretraining in conjunction with instruction tuning offer accessible method of specializing large language models to given domain such as additive manufacturing.

sections.title: 7 Future Work
sections.content: AdditiveLLM2 explores the methods of domain adaptive pretraining and instruction tuning of the Gemma 3 model 35 to the field of additive manufacturing. Future work would explore extending the usage of this model with agentic systems, enhanced datasets, and continual learning environments. The most immediate application would investigate applying AdditiveLLM2 into an agentic system, evaluating the efficiency with which it is capable of making the appropriate tool calls using its adapted domain knowledge. The performance of this system would be evaluated in manner similar to that of LLM-3D Print 24 where images of the process in-situ are evaluated using the VLM component of the agentic system and the appropriate actions are taken to address potential issues. Building upon the discussions in Section 5.2 regarding domain specialization performance, domain adaptation pretraining and instruction tuning with more comprehensive dataset would be worth investigating. Although the dataset used in this work was sufficient to exhibit performance gains across many benchmarking tasks, larger and more encompassing dataset of additive manufacturing processes would result greater performance gains. Further specialization to specific task (i.e. anomaly detection within laser powder bed fusion) is expected to be reflected in task performance, however, this may impact performance on other tasks. To this, the concept of continual learning 75 will be further investigated and applied to the domain adaptation process to better retain trained abilities in subsequent training and fine-tuning cycles. This will alleviate the effects of catastrophic forgetting, 107 building to framework with which the agentic system can integrate the results of its actions into the LLM through fine-tuning.

sections.title: Appendix A Tokenization
sections.content: Tokenization is an essential component of the Natural Language Processing (NLP) pipeline as it converts strings of human-readable characters into token representations which are then embedded into vectors for the large language model. 108110 Raw text does not provide suitable representation medium for models to train upon as it commands large vocabulary and treats words as distinct units. 108 Thus, tokenization presents more efficient representation of the data to the model as an embedding vector. 50 Tokenization methods include dividing character strings into word and subword units (Appendix A.1) along with indexing frequently occuring sequences detected using Byte Pair Encoding (Appendix A.2). 108 In order to retain positional data, methods such as sinusoidal positional encoding 50 or Rotary Position Embeddings (RoPE) 111 are added to the token embedding vectors. By converting the tokens to vector embeddings with positional data, the model is able to use the semantic and sequential patterns of the input to perform next token prediction from the representations learned during training. 50,108,111 Appendix A.1 Subword Neural Machine Translation Subword Neural Machine Translation is preprocessing method which text is segmented into subword units, specifically useful in encoding out-of-vocabulary (OOV) words. The approach proposed by Sennrich et al. 108 implements an adapted version of Byte Pair Encoding 112 (BPE) further discussed in Appendix A.2 to first generate the pair table for frequently occurring character sequences within the train text. This is similar to the pair table seen in the compressed output that original BPE produces, however with slight adjustment of merging characters rather than bytes in order to suit the application of word segmentation. 108 Along with this, the compression routine is set to conclude after given number of operations rather than the original BPE process of repeating until there are no more remaining bytes in the text. 112 This provides tunable num operations parameter which balances the frequency 30 for complete words and subwords within the dictionary, improving the coverage of tokens during training. This allows for out-of-vocabulary words to be segmented into combinations of word and subword tokens. Appendix A.2 Byte Pair Encoding Byte Pair Encoding was first introduced by Philip Gage 112 as method of data compression useful in memory constrained environments due to its fast expansion routine. The compression routine of the algorithm looks for most adjacent byte pairs that occur most frequently within given pass and replaces the pair with byte that doesnt already exist within the data. This repeats until there is either no more frequent byte pairs or there are no more remaining unused bytes. 112 The expansion routine is performed over single pass over the input file, where byte literals are passed directly to the output buffer and byte pairs are pushed onto stack. Within each iteration, if the stack contains data the byte there is used as the next input byte, otherwise the next input byte is obtained from the input file. This is the content for the first appendix.
